title: How to Create a Financial Practice That Includes Your Whole Story
body:
Your Money Story Isn't Just Numbers. It's Your Life.

Truth is, most of you are carrying around a heavy, unspoken burden about money. It's not just the debt you owe, or the savings you don't have. It's the shame, the fear, the generational baggage that makes every financial decision feel like a test you're destined to fail. You think you're bad with money. You're not. You're just disconnected from your true self when it comes to your finances. You've been told to budget, to cut expenses, to follow rigid rules that ignore the messy, beautiful reality of who you are. And it's not working, is it? Know what I mean? We're going to talk about creating a financial practice that actually includes *you*. Not some idealized version of you, but the real, flawed, magnificent you. This isn't about quick fixes or magic formulas. It's about deep, honest work. It's about looking at your money not as a separate entity, but as an extension of your entire being. What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It often is. It reflects back to you all your fears, your hopes, your insecurities, your deepest desires. It's time to stop fighting with it and start listening.

The Lie of "Just Budget It"

You've heard it a thousand times: "Just make a budget!" "Cut out the lattes!" "Live below your means!" And sure, there's a kernel of truth in responsible spending. But for so many of you, these pronouncements land like a judgment. They feel prescriptive, devoid of context, and frankly, a bit insulting. They imply that if you're struggling, it's because you're either stupid or undisciplined. This approach, often championed by folks like Dave Ramsey, is shame-based. It works for some, yes. The ones who are already wired for strict adherence, or who are so deep in crisis they'll grab onto anything. But for the vast majority, it creates more shame, more guilt, and a deeper sense of failure when you inevitably "mess up." Why? Because it completely ignores your history. It ignores your childhood experiences with money - the scarcity, the fights, the unspoken rules. It ignores the cultural narratives you've absorbed. It ignores your temperament, your values, your dreams. It assumes you're a robot, capable of simply inputting numbers and following commands. You're not. You're a complex human being. And your financial life is just as complex as your emotional life, your relational life, your spiritual life. They're all intertwined. You can't just isolate one piece and expect to "fix" it without addressing the whole. Think about it. You can track every penny, use every budgeting app under the sun, and still feel completely out of control. Why? Because the underlying beliefs, the unconscious patterns, the emotional triggers, they're still running the show. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It doesn't care if you have a budget. It's a deeply ingrained belief system that keeps you feeling like there's never enough, or that you don't deserve what you have, or that it'll all be taken away. This isn't about logic; it's about conditioning.

Your Whole Story: Beyond the Ledger

So, what does it mean to include your whole story? It means understanding that your relationship with money didn't start the day you got your first paycheck. It started much, much earlier. It started with observing your parents, hearing their anxieties, internalizing their beliefs. It started with your first experience of not having something you wanted, or having something taken away. Lynne Twist, in her powerful book The Art of Money (paid link), talks about how our relationship with money is a reflection of our relationship with life itself. It's about scarcity, sufficiency, and true abundance. She challenges the idea that there's not enough, that we're somehow separate from the flow of resources. This isn't just fluffy spiritual talk. It's a deep psychological insight. When you believe there's not enough, you operate from a place of fear and contraction. You hoard, you hide, you feel guilty when you spend. Or, you spend impulsively, trying to fill an internal void. To bring your whole story into your financial practice, you need to become an archaeologist of your own financial past. * **What did you hear about money growing up?** Was it a source of stress? A secret? A tool for power? * **What emotions come up when you think about money?** Fear? Anger? Resentment? Hope? Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Does your stomach clench? Do your shoulders rise? * **What messages did you internalize about your worth and money?** Do you believe you have to suffer to earn it? Do you believe rich people are inherently bad? Do you believe you're not smart enough to manage it? These aren't rhetorical questions. These are journal prompts. These are conversations you need to have with yourself, perhaps with a trusted friend or therapist. This is where the real work begins. You can't change what you don't acknowledge.

The Power of Conscious Awareness

Once you start unearthing these stories, you begin to see how they've been dictating your financial choices. You see the patterns. Maybe you overspend when you feel stressed, a coping mechanism learned from a parent who shopped to de-stress. Maybe you hoard money, terrified of a scarcity you experienced in childhood. Maybe you sabotage your own success because deep down, you don't feel worthy of wealth. This is where conscious awareness becomes your superpower. Sam Harris, the neuroscientist and philosopher, talks extensively about the illusion of the self and the power of mindfulness. While he's not directly discussing money, his work is incredibly relevant. When you can observe your thoughts and emotions around money without judgment, you create a space between the stimulus (a bill, a desire to spend) and your reaction. You interrupt the automatic programming. You gain agency. This isn't about willpower. It's about awareness. It's about presence. Kate Northrup, in her book Mind Over Money (paid link), advocates for aligning your money with your values and creating a system that supports your life, rather than constraining it. She understands that money isn't just a spreadsheet; it's a tool for creating the life you desire. But you can't create that life if you're operating from unconscious fears and outdated beliefs. So, how do you cultivate this conscious awareness? 1. **Mindful Money Check-ins:** Instead of dreading checking your bank account, approach it as a neutral observer. Just look at the numbers. Notice your breath. Notice any physical sensations. No judgment, just observation. 2. **Journaling:** Write down your feelings about money, your fears, your hopes. Don't edit yourself. Just let it flow. This is a powerful way to externalize those internal narratives. 3. **Meditation:** Even five minutes a day can help you develop the ability to observe your thoughts without getting swept away by them. This skill is invaluable when financial anxiety strikes. This isn't just about feeling better. It's about making better decisions from a place of clarity, not reactivity. You feel that?

Integrity and Values: Your True North

Your whole story also includes your values. What truly matters to you? Is it freedom? Security? Contribution? Creativity? Family? Adventure? Often, our spending habits are completely misaligned with our stated values. We say family is important, but we work 80 hours a week to buy things we don't need, sacrificing time with them. We say health is important, but we skimp on good food and gym memberships. A financial practice that includes your whole story is one that is deeply rooted in your personal integrity. It's about aligning your money with what you truly believe in. This means: * **Conscious Spending:** Before you buy something, ask yourself: Does this align with my values? Am I buying this from a place of abundance or scarcity? Is this genuinely going to enhance my life, or am I trying to fill a void? * **Conscious Earning:** Does your work align with your values? Are you earning money in a way that feels authentic and fulfilling? Or are you sacrificing your soul for a paycheck? This is a big question, and it's not always easy to answer or to change. But acknowledging the misalignment is the first step. * **Conscious Giving:** Giving isn't just for the wealthy. It's a powerful practice of abundance, showing yourself and the universe that there *is* enough. It connects you to a larger purpose. It can be time, talent, or treasure. It doesn't have to be a lot, but it has to be intentional. When you bring your values into your financial decisions, money stops being this abstract, scary thing and becomes a tool. A very powerful tool for building the life you want. It's about intentionality, not deprivation.

Healing the Wounds: Self-Compassion and Imperfection

This journey isn't always easy. You're going to uncover some uncomfortable truths. You're going to make mistakes. You're going to fall back into old patterns. And that's okay. This is precisely where self-compassion comes in. Brené Brown, in The Gifts of Imperfection (paid link), teaches us about the power of vulnerability and self-acceptance. When it comes to money, many of us feel immense shame about our past choices, our current situation, our perceived failures. This shame keeps us stuck. It prevents us from seeking help, from being honest with ourselves, from moving forward. You have to be kind to yourself. You have to acknowledge that you're doing the best you can with the tools you have. And if those tools are faulty - if they're based on old fears and outdated beliefs - then it's not your fault they're not working. It's simply an invitation to learn new tools. When you catch yourself in a negative money pattern, instead of berating yourself, try this: * **Acknowledge the feeling:** "I notice I'm feeling really anxious about this bill." * **Connect to the underlying story:** "This anxiety reminds me of when my parents used to fight about money." * **Offer yourself compassion:** "It makes sense that I feel this way. That was a really difficult time. But I'm an adult now, and I have more resources." * **Choose a new action:** "What's one small, conscious step I can take right now?" This isn't about ignoring your problems. It's about approaching them from a place of self-support, not self-attack. Shame is a terrible motivator. Compassion, on the other hand, is a powerful catalyst for change. Right?!

Creating Your Practice: Small, Consistent Steps

So, how do you actually *do* this? It's not about throwing out all your budgets or financial tools. It's about infusing them with consciousness and self-awareness. It's about making them *yours*. 1. **The Money Date:** Schedule a regular "money date" with yourself. Once a week, once a month - whatever works. This isn't a chore. It's a sacred time to connect with your finances. Review your accounts, pay bills, look at your goals. But here's the crucial part: do it with awareness. Light a candle. Play some calming music. Treat it like a meditation. Notice your feelings. Journal about them. 2. **Tracking with Intention:** Track your spending, yes. But not just to judge yourself. Track it to understand your patterns. Where does your money *actually* go? Does it align with your values? Where are the leaks? Where are the unconscious expenditures? This isn't about guilt-tripping yourself into deprivation. It's about gaining clarity. 3. **Envision Your Future:** What does a financially healthy, aligned life look like for you? Don't just think about numbers. Think about feelings. What does it *feel* like to be financially secure? To have enough? To be generous? Create a vision board. Write it down. Feel it in your body. This isn't wishful thinking; it's conscious creation. 4. **Practice Gratitude:** Even if you're struggling, find something to be grateful for regarding money. The roof over your head. The food in your fridge. The job you have. The ability to buy a coffee. Gratitude shifts your energetic state from scarcity to sufficiency. It opens you up to more. 5. **Seek Support:** You don't have to do this alone. Talk to a trusted friend. Find a financial therapist. Join a conscious money group. The shame around money thrives in isolation. When you bring it into the light, it loses its power. This isn't a race. It's a lifelong practice. There will be good days and bad days. The goal isn't perfection; it's presence. It's about continually coming back to yourself, to your values, to your whole story, and allowing that to guide your financial decisions. It's about reclaiming your power and your peace around money.

FAQ

What if I have a lot of debt? Can this approach still help me?

Absolutely. Especially if you have a lot of debt. Debt often carries the heaviest emotional weight. This approach helps you address the shame and fear associated with debt, which are often bigger hurdles than the actual numbers. Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. By understanding the underlying stories and emotions connected to your debt, you can approach repayment from a place of agency and self-compassion, rather than self-punishment. It's not about ignoring the debt; it's about healing your relationship with it so you can effectively tackle it.

Is this just "manifesting" or "positive thinking" about money?

No, it's much deeper than that. While positive thinking can be a component, this approach is grounded in psychological and spiritual principles. It's about deep self-inquiry, conscious awareness, and aligning your actions with your values. It's not about wishing for money to appear, but about understanding your internal world around money and making intentional choices from that place. It's about shifting your *relationship* with money, which then naturally leads to different behaviors and outcomes. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It's a deeply ingrained pattern, and this work helps to dismantle it.

How long does it take to see results with this kind of financial practice?

This isn't a quick fix; it's a lifelong journey of self-discovery and conscious living. Some shifts might feel immediate - a sense of relief, a moment of clarity. Other, deeper changes will unfold over time, as you consistently practice awareness and self-compassion. The "results" aren't just about your bank balance, though that often improves too. The real results are internal: more peace, less anxiety, greater integrity, and a deeper sense of connection to yourself and your life. What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? This practice helps you embrace that honesty.

Today, I challenge you to schedule your first "money date." Even if it's just 15 minutes. Sit down, open your banking app, and simply observe. No judgment. Just witness. Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover how to build a financial practice that honors your unique life experiences. Learn to connect with your money story, overcome burdens, and make empowere
author: Kalesh
